summarization.a: The bar chart shows the etch rate per cycle for the individual half-reactions (Reaction A only and Reaction B only) compared with the full ALE cycle. Reaction A alone produces no measurable etch, Reaction B (AlGaN) alone causes only a small background etch (~0.07 Å/cycle), while the combined ALE process yields ~0.807 Å/cycle, giving a  synergy of ~91%.
summarization.b: The bar chart shows the etch rate per cycle for the individual half-reactions (Reaction A only and Reaction B only) compared with the full ALE cycle. Reaction A alone produces no measurable etch, Reaction B (GaN) alone causes only a small background etch (~0.07 Å/cycle), while the combined ALE process yields ~2.639 Å/cycle, giving a  synergy of ~97%.

data_extraction.a:
| Etch rate (Å/cycle): Reaction A (240 cycles) | Etch rate (Å/cycle):ALE (100 Cycles) | Etch rate (Å/cycle):Reaction B (400 cycles) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.000 | 0.807 | 0.07 |
data_extraction.b:
| Etch rate (Å/cycle):Reaction A (240 cycles) | Etch rate (Å/cycle):ALE (72 Cycles) | Etch rate (Å/cycle):Reaction B (400 cycles) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.000 | 2.639 | 0.07 |

b.question: If reaction's A etch rate were exactly the same as the etch rate of reaction B, what would happen to the synergy value and why?
b.answer: Synergy would decrease. By definition, synergy is calculated by subtracting the sum of the two single-step etch rates from the full ALE rate. Numerically, it would be: (2.639-0.14)/2.639 = 94.6%.

b.question: Which process is closer to an ideal ALE? AlGaN has a synergy of ~91%.
b.answer: The GaN process is closer to ideal ALE, because its synergy is higher.
